Baal-zebub evolves into a name for Satan or prince of demons in Christian texts2

Originally a Philistine deity, Beelzebub (derived from Baal-zebub) became associated with Satan in the New Testament. This demonization occurred as early Christians sought to discredit pagan deities1.

Initially referring to the morning star or Venus, Lucifer became associated with Satan due to a particular interpretation of Isaiah 14:12. Church fathers like Origen and Jerome popularized this connection1.
While Baal was a prominent Canaanite deity, early Christian writers began associating him with demonic forces, viewing pagan gods as demons in disguise1.
Originally a Persian demon, Asmodeus entered Jewish and Christian demonology through apocryphal texts like the Book of Tobit. He became associated with lust and matrimonial discord1.

Though not originally a name but an Aramaic word for "wealth," Mammon became personified as a demon of greed during the Middle Ages, likely due to increasing concerns about materialism in Christian society1.
Originally associated with the Knights Templar, Baphomet became demonized during the witch hunts of the early modern period, conflated with Satan in popular imagination1.
In early Hebrew texts, "the satan" is a title meaning "the accuser," not yet associated with evil3.
Satan appears as an accuser in God's court, testing Job's faith13.
